---
title: Merging datasets without integration
date: 2024-12-23
metadata-files: 
  - _metadata.yml
  - ../_quarto.yml
---

## Goal of this analysis

To demonstrate the need for integrating the four human and mouse datasets (human, Aldinger and Sepp; mouse, Sepp and Vladoiu) to remove the "species" batch effect instead of just merging them.

## Files and directories

### Code

The code can be found `<integrated software directory>/merge_datasets.R` and run using the `scrnaseq_env` conda environment. Briefly, the merging workflow is as follows:

1. Use `SCTransform` to normalize each dataset individuallly (already performed by Leo, the script just loads these datasets)
1. Merge the datasets (with the `merge` function)
1. Because the top variable features are lost after merging, we need to set them again by running `SelectIntegrationFeatures`
1. Correct for normalization differences with PrepSCTFindMarkers
1. Run PCA and use top 50 PCs for the UMAP

See the following GitHub threads for more information on the workflow:

- https://github.com/satijalab/seurat/issues/5738#issuecomment-1169321584
- https://github.com/satijalab/seurat/issues/6185
- https://github.com/satijalab/seurat/issues/7407

### Output

The output directory can be found in `<integrated results directory>/20241223`.

- `merged_seurat.qs` contains the Seurat object with the merged datasets


## Results

::: {#fig-pca layout-nrow=2}
![](/.mounts/labs/pailab/private/icheong/CBL_scRNAseq/results/integrated/20241223/dataset_pca.png)

![](/.mounts/labs/pailab/private/icheong/CBL_scRNAseq/results/integrated/20241223/species_pca.png)

PCAs of the merged datasets with cells coloured by (A) dataset and (B) species.
:::

::: {#fig-umap layout-nrow=2}
![](/.mounts/labs/pailab/private/icheong/CBL_scRNAseq/results/integrated/20241223/dataset_umap.png)

![](/.mounts/labs/pailab/private/icheong/CBL_scRNAseq/results/integrated/20241223/species_umap.png)

UMAPs of the merged datasets with cells coloured by (A) dataset and (B) species.
:::

As expected, there appears to be a significant amount of batch effect when merging the datasets, thereby suggesting a need for integration. The batch effect between the two mouse datasets (Sepp/Vladoiu) is much more pronounced than between the two human datasets (Aldinger/Sepp) (Figure [@fig-umap]A).
